title: Boost Inference Performance up to 15x on NVIDIA Blackwell Using DFlash Speculative Decoding | NVIDIA Technical Blog
gist: DFlash, an open-source block-diffusion speculative decoding model from UC San Diego, drafts entire token blocks in parallel to deliver up to 15x throughput for gpt-oss-120b on NVIDIA Blackwell and 1.5x over EAGLE-3, with checkpoints on Hugging Face and integration into SGLang, vLLM, and TensorRT-LLM requiring no application refactoring.

insights.claim: DFlash replaces the autoregressive drafter in speculative decoding with a lightweight block-diffusion drafter that predicts a block of masked future tokens in a single forward pass, turning sequential drafting into block-parallel GPU work while preserving the target model's output distribution through verification.
insights.speaker: Amr Elmeleegy
insights.so_what: Parallel block drafting attacks the sequential bottleneck of autoregressive generation, the core throughput limiter in latency-sensitive serving.

insights.claim: DFlash combines three techniques: block-diffusion drafting that predicts multiple future tokens in parallel, target hidden-state conditioning using context features from the target model, and KV injection that injects target context features into the draft model's key-value projections across layers to maintain high acceptance rates.
insights.speaker: Amr Elmeleegy
insights.so_what: KV injection and hidden-state conditioning are what keep acceptance rates high; without them parallel drafting would not preserve quality.

insights.claim: At the high interactivity range of 500-600 tokens/sec per user, DFlash increases throughput on NVIDIA Blackwell by more than 15x compared with autoregressive decoding and 1.5x higher than EAGLE-3 speculative decoding for gpt-oss-120b served across eight NVIDIA DGX B300 GPUs using TensorRT-LLM.
insights.speaker: Amr Elmeleegy
insights.so_what: 15x throughput at fixed interactivity directly lowers cost-per-token for agentic coding workloads, a key serving-economics lever.

insights.claim: DFlash delivers up to 5.8x higher throughput over autoregressive decoding on Gemma 4 31B via vLLM on a single Blackwell Ultra GPU and up to 5.1x on Qwen3 8-B via SGLang on a single B200 GPU, both at concurrency 1.
insights.speaker: Amr Elmeleegy
insights.so_what: Multi-x gains extend to smaller models and single GPUs, broadening DFlash's applicability beyond flagship multi-GPU deployments.
insights.locator: On Gemma 4 31B running on a single Blackwell

insights.claim: Each NVIDIA Blackwell Ultra GPU combines two reticle-sized dies connected by 10 TB/s chip-to-chip interconnect, forming a unified compute domain with 160 SMs, 640 fifth-generation Tensor Cores, and 15 PFLOPS of dense NVFP4 compute, which DFlash exploits to serve up to 15x more users concurrently at the same interactivity rate.
insights.speaker: Amr Elmeleegy
insights.so_what: Hardware spec detail underscores that DFlash gains are tied to Blackwell's parallel compute, reinforcing NVIDIA hardware-software co-design lock-in.
insights.locator: Each NVIDIA Blackwell Ultra GPU combines

insights.claim: Researchers at UC San Diego released the DFlash paper in February 2026, and the team has since released 20 DFlash model checkpoints on Hugging Face with NVIDIA Blackwell and Hopper recipes covering Qwen, Kimi K2.6, Llama, Gemma, and gpt-oss model families.
insights.speaker: Amr Elmeleegy
insights.so_what: Broad checkpoint coverage across model families signals fast real-world adoptability, not just a research demo.
insights.locator: Researchers at UC San Diego released the paper

insights.claim: On vLLM developers can swap EAGLE-3 with a DFlash checkpoint with no code changes outside the config, running through the open-source Speculators library, while SGLang migration only requires updating the speculative decoding algorithm and providing the matching DFlash draft checkpoint.
insights.speaker: Amr Elmeleegy
insights.so_what: Zero-refactor drop-in adoption removes switching costs, accelerating displacement of EAGLE-3 as the default speculative decoder.
insights.locator: On vLLM, developers can swap EAGLE-3
